name: Day of the Lord
summary: The sacred scriptures and writings that form the foundation of Christian faith and theology.
structure: Prophetic Development - Joel's Day of the Lord in Revelation's apocalypse
influence: Connected Pentecost to end times; shaped eschatological theology
icon:

description: Day of the Lord is an important sacred text in christian tradition.
author: Joel (Prophet), John (Apostle), Peter (Apostle)

keyMyths.title: Cosmic Upheaval and Divine Judgment
keyMyths.description: The Day of the Lord is frequently associated with cosmic disturbances such as earthquakes, darkened skies, and celestial phenomena, signaling the arrival of God's judgment upon the wicked and the vindication of the righteous. It represents a period of divine intervention and reckoning.

keyMyths.title: The Gathering of Nations for Judgment
keyMyths.description: Many prophetic texts depict a scenario where God gathers all nations to the Valley of Jehoshaphat (or a symbolic location) for judgment on the Day of the Lord. This event signifies God's ultimate sovereignty over all peoples and the accountability of nations.

keyMyths.title: Purification and Redemption of Israel
keyMyths.description: The Day of the Lord is not solely about judgment; it also encompasses the purification and restoration of Israel. God promises to cleanse His people from their sins, remove idolatry, and establish a renewed covenant with them, ushering in an era of righteousness.

keyMyths.title: The Coming of Elijah as a Forerunner
keyMyths.description: Malachi prophesies the return of Elijah before the great and terrible Day of the Lord. Elijah's role is to turn the hearts of the fathers to their children and the hearts of the children to their fathers, averting a curse upon the land.

keyMyths.title: Christ's Second Coming and Final Judgment
keyMyths.description: In the New Testament, the Day of the Lord is often linked to Christ's second coming, when He will judge the living and the dead. This event signifies the culmination of God's plan of salvation and the establishment of His eternal kingdom.

sources.name: The Holy Bible (Various Translations)
sources.description: Primary source containing numerous references to the Day of the Lord across the Old and New Testaments.
sources.title: The Holy Bible (Various Translations)

sources.name: John Calvin, Institutes of the Christian Religion
sources.description: A systematic theological work providing Calvin's interpretation of biblical prophecy, including the Day of the Lord.
sources.title: John Calvin, Institutes of the Christian Religion

sources.name: G.K. Beale, The Book of Revelation: A Shorter Commentary
sources.description: An academic commentary providing detailed analysis of Revelation's apocalyptic imagery and its relation to the Day of the Lord.
sources.title: G.K. Beale, The Book of Revelation: A Shorter Commentary

sources.name: N.T. Wright, Surprised by Hope: Rethinking Heaven, the Resurrection, and the Mission of the Church
sources.description: A theological exploration of eschatology, including discussions of the Day of the Lord and its implications for Christian hope.
sources.title: N.T. Wright, Surprised by Hope: Rethinking Heaven, the Resurrection, and the Mission of the Church

sources.name: Gerhard von Rad, Old Testament Theology
sources.description: A comprehensive analysis of Old Testament theology, with sections addressing prophetic pronouncements about the Day of the Lord.
sources.title: Gerhard von Rad, Old Testament Theology
